notes.anchor: the most assured and most rightful triumph
notes.note: The triumph was the formal victory procession through Rome granted by the Senate to a victorious commander; Appius had hoped for one for his campaigns as governor of Cilicia, but his enemies' prosecutions cost him the honour.

notes.anchor: of a commander by his weight
notes.note: Cicero, as the sitting governor (imperator) of Cilicia, the very province Appius had just left, offers to throw the authority of his office behind Appius's defence.

The censorship was the most prestigious Roman magistracy, held every five years: the censors enrolled citizens, let public contracts, and policed senatorial morals (the regimen morum). Appius was standing for it and was elected censor for 50 BC.

Phrygia Epictetus ("Acquired Phrygia"), a district of Phrygia in the province of Asia attached to Cicero's command; its communities had complained of the cost of the embassies they were asked to fund.

A statute of Sulla capping the travelling allowances a province could vote to embassies sent to Rome; Cicero says he merely advised the towns to keep their grants close to this legal ceiling, not below it.

notes.anchor: the most ample college of priests
notes.note: The college of augurs, to which both Cicero and Appius belonged; by its rule a man at enmity with a member could not be co-opted, so membership was itself a pledge of friendship between augurs.
Pompey's son Gnaeus had married Appius's daughter, making Pompey and Appius connections by marriage (adfinitas); Cicero leans on his own debt to Pompey to bind himself to Appius.
A contemptuous reference to the provincial subjects of Asia Minor; Cicero reproaches Appius for trusting the malicious talk of such low informants, named here for their proverbial unreliability.
